experiences.intentions:
Player 1: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior information, I know almost nothing certain about others' roles. The proposed 2-player team excludes me but includes the proposer, which is normal early. On quest 1, approving a reasonable opening team is standard because rejecting gives little info and can stall good teams. My vote reveals only that I'm willing to test an initial team; that does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or votes. As Good Servant on round 1, a 2-person team of proposer plus one other is standard and provides early information with low downside; rejecting without evidence is less useful this early. Approving reveals some voting data and, if it goes, quest result data for future reads.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 is evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil player, so approving would risk an immediate failed quest and gives bad team composition. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject teams containing known evil, while not making my knowledge too explicit beyond standard suspicion. Rejecting helps good by preventing an evil-influenced quest and reveals only a reasonable anti-team stance.
Player 2: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,4 are unknown good roles. The proposed team is [0,2], which includes me and excludes my teammate. In round 1, as evil, approving a team with myself on it is strong because I can choose to fail the quest if needed and an approve vote on my own proposal looks natural. This reveals little since leaders often back their own teams, and rejecting my own team would be suspicious and hurt evil's chance to control quest 1.
Player 3: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so the proposed team [0,2] contains one evil player and excludes me. On quest 1 with a 2-player team, having exactly one evil on it gives evil the option to fail immediately. As evil, I generally want early quest control and to normalize approving teams with player 2. Approving also reveals little because many players approve round-1 teams; rejecting could unnecessarily distance me from a strong evil team and make 2 look worse if the team later passes without my support.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin, so I know 2 and 3 are evil-visible players. The current phase is quest voting, and I am on the team [0,2]. As a Good player, optimal play is always to pass; failing would directly harm Good and be illegal for my alignment. My pass reveals no special information beyond standard Good behavior and avoids giving away Merlin-specific reads.
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate; others are unknown. On quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,2], an early fail immediately creates suspicion on me as proposer and quest member, while a pass preserves cover for both evil players and keeps team reads muddled. As Minion, optimal play here is usually to pass the first quest when I’m the only evil on a 2-person team, especially in round 1, to avoid exposing myself too early. This reveals less concrete info and helps evil more long-term by maintaining my credibility for future teams.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] definitely includes an evil player, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids sending a bad team and signals suspicion of 2 without overexplaining; approving would help Evil.
Player 4: I'm Good Servant. Team [0,2] succeeded on Quest 1, while [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, so player 3 is the strongest evil suspect. Keeping 3 off is optimal. Either 0 or 2 could still be evil who chose to pass Q1, but [0,2] is currently the best available 2-player team. Approving preserves a team that excludes the main suspect and reveals whether this pair is clean on Quest 3.
Player 2: I know player 3 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, which could have been me or 3, so it does not clear 0. Current proposed team [0,2] excludes my known evil teammate and includes me; as evil, approving gives us a chance to sabotage quest 3 directly. Even though approving may give some info if the quest fails, a fail here puts evil at match point, which is optimal.
Player 1: I’m Good with limited info. Team [0,2] succeeded on Quest 1, while [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, which makes Player 3 the strongest evil candidate since 0 and 2 were on the prior successful 2-person team. Re-approving [0,2] is optimal because it excludes the main suspect and preserves the best-known good pair. My vote reveals trust in the strongest available evidence and helps Good coordinate around the likely safe team.
Player 3: I know player 2 is evil with me. Team [0,2] excludes me after the failed [0,2,3] where exactly one fail appeared, which could implicate either 2 or me to others; if this 2-person team goes and fails, suspicion on 2 increases sharply. As evil on a team vote, optimal play is usually to approve teams containing an evil player, since we get a chance to fail and can still manage suspicion later. My vote itself reveals little because team votes are aggregated and approving aligns with wanting evil on mission.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,4] excludes both known evil players, which is exactly what Good wants on quest 4 while down 1-2. Approving also avoids risking worse teams later and gives Good the best chance to tie. Voting approve is consistent with good play and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting an all-good team.
Player 4: I’m Good with no hidden info. From results: team [0,2] succeeded once and later failed once, so at least one of 0 or 2 is Evil. Team [0,2,3] also failed, which is consistent with 0 or 2 being Evil; 3 is less cleared. Proposed team [0,1,4] excludes 2 and includes me plus untested 1. Given 0 appears on every prior team and at least one of 0/2 is Evil, this team is highly suspicious because it keeps 0 while dropping 2. As Good in team voting on quest 4 when Evil already has 2 fails, optimal play is to reject teams likely containing suspected Evil. Rejecting reveals resistance to a suspicious 0-centered team and avoids handing Evil a likely win.
Player 3: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 2. Team [0,1,4] contains no known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives Good momentum. Prior quests with teams containing 2 and/or me produced one success and two fails, which likely makes 0 somewhat trusted but doesn't clear 1 or 4. As Evil, on quest 4 I want to reject all-good teams and push for inclusion of 2 or 3 on a future team. My vote itself reveals little since rejecting a team with no suspected evil is consistent with many worlds, and preserving flexibility helps Evil.
Player 1: Player 2 was on all first three quests; Q1 with [0,2] succeeded, then Q2 [0,2,3] failed, and Q3 [0,2] failed. Since [0,2] went from success to fail, at least one of 0 or 2 is evil, and Q2 adds possibility 3 but Q3 strongly points to 0/2. Team [0,1,4] excludes player 2 and 3, but still includes 0 who is suspicious. As Good servant at 1, with evil already at 2 wins, I should reject teams containing a likely evil when there is no strong reason to trust 0. Rejecting reveals caution and is better than risking immediate evil win on quest 4.
Player 2: I know player 3 is evil with me. Teams containing 2 succeeded once and then failed twice with exactly one fail, which implicates either me or 3 to others; this current team [0,1,4] excludes both known evils, so it is likely all-good. In team voting on quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins, optimal evil play is to approve a good-looking team if possible to preserve my cover, but since this team likely succeeds and would tie the game 2-2, rejecting is better because I want to block a probable success and there are still multiple rounds left before auto-pass. My reject signals suspicion of this team, which is acceptable since evil benefits from preventing a good quest and I’m already somewhat implicated from prior teams.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [1,2,3] contains both evil players 2 and 3, guaranteeing we can fail quest 4 and likely win immediately. From prior results, good may suspect 0 or 4 due to past failed teams, so approving this strong evil team is optimal. My vote reveals little because evil and some good could both plausibly approve; rejecting would unnecessarily risk the team being changed.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 2 and 3 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains two known evil players, so as Good I should strongly reject it. Approving would risk an immediate evil win on quest 4 and reveals little beyond my opposition to a bad team, which is acceptable even if it may make me slightly suspicious later.
Player 3: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and I am 3, so this proposed team [1,2,3] contains two evil players. That is excellent for securing the 4th quest fail and winning immediately, since on 5-player Avalon only one fail is needed on quest 4. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to approve strong evil-controlled teams. Approving is also natural here because player 1 is proposing a team that includes two prior suspicious players after two fails, and if it passes we can end the game on the quest. My approve vote reveals little because both good and evil could plausibly support this team.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Quest 2 team [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil. Quest 3 team [0,2] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/2 is evil. Combining these, player 3 could be good while exactly one of 0 or 2 is evil; but if 3 were evil then one of 0/2 would also still be evil. Team [1,2,3] excludes 0, who is one strong suspect from being on both failed teams. It includes 1, who was not on any failed quest and proposed this team. Since Good is down 1-2, rejecting too many teams is risky, but this team is plausibly better than alternatives and gives info on whether 2/3 are implicated. Approving helps avoid auto-pass pressure and tests a team with only two carryover suspects instead of repeating 0.
Player 1: I am Good and need to avoid sending likely evil players. Quest 2 [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil. Quest 3 [0,2] also had exactly one fail, which strongly implies one of 0 or 2 is evil; combined with the prior result, 3 is somewhat less likely than 0/2 but still uncertain. Team [1,2,3] removes 0, the strongest suspect from appearing on both failed teams with 2, but still includes 2 and 3 where at least one may be evil. However, as a 3-person Quest 4 in 5-player Avalon still fails on one fail, this team is risky and not well justified from known info. Rejecting preserves chance for a better team like including 1 with one of 3/4 and excluding 0/2. My vote reveals skepticism of teams containing 2/3 based on evidence, which helps Good coordinate.
